description: Simplify the permission UI in the side panel. Replace all native <select> elements with the RetroUI Select component (already added at platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/retro/Select.tsx). Remove the group-level Switch toggle — group headers become plain visual dividers. Make the plugin-level select set the plugin default permission (which all tools inherit) rather than batch-writing every tool individually. Make the tool-level select smart: when a tool's permission matches the plugin default, remove the per-tool override from config (keeping config minimal). The plugin-level permission field on PluginPermissionConfig stays — it is the default that tools inherit from.

userStories.title: Create PermissionSelect reusable component using RetroUI Select
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a compact PermissionSelect component that wraps the RetroUI Select for the off/ask/auto permission values so that it can be reused in ToolRow, PluginCard, and BrowserToolsCard
acceptanceCriteria: PermissionSelect component exists in ToolRow.tsx (co-located) and is exported
It renders a RetroUI Select (from ./retro/Select.js) with three items: Off, Ask, Auto
The trigger is compact: h-6, w-[4.5rem], px-1.5, py-0, font-mono text-xs, shadow-none, with a focus shadow matching the retro theme
It accepts props: value (ToolPermission), onValueChange ((value: ToolPermission) => void), disabled (boolean), ariaLabel (string)
The trigger has onClick stopPropagation to prevent accordion toggle when clicking inside a card header
The Select.Content uses font-mono text-xs to match the trigger
Storybook story shows all three states (off, ask, auto) and disabled state
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run test passes

userStories.title: Replace native select in ToolRow with PermissionSelect
userStories.description: As a user, I want the tool permission selector to use the RetroUI Select component for visual consistency with the design system
acceptanceCriteria: ToolRow renders PermissionSelect instead of the native <select> element
The PermissionSelect receives the tool's permission value, the change handler, disabled state, and aria label
Visual appearance matches the retro theme (border-2, border-border, font-mono, flat shadow on focus)
ToolRow.stories.tsx updated to show all three permission states using PermissionSelect
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/ToolRow.tsx — Replace the native <select> (lines 44-54) with PermissionSelect. The change handler calls onPermissionChange(name, newPermission).
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/ToolRow.stories.tsx — Update stories if needed.

userStories.title: Replace native select in PluginCard header with PermissionSelect and remove group Switch
userStories.description: As a user, I want the plugin-level permission selector to use RetroUI Select and group headers to be plain text dividers without toggle switches
acceptanceCriteria: PluginCard header renders PermissionSelect (imported from ToolRow) instead of native <select>
The PermissionSelect value is plugin.permission (the plugin-level default from the server)
Changing the plugin-level permission calls setPluginPermission (existing bridge method) which sets the plugin-level default — it does NOT batch-write every tool individually
The Switch import is removed from PluginCard
Group headers render as plain text dividers: just the group name label with bg-muted/20 styling, no Switch component, no toggle handler
handleToggleGroup function is removed entirely
PluginCard.stories.tsx updated: remove any references to group toggle behavior
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/PluginCard.tsx:
  1. Import PermissionSelect from './ToolRow.js' instead of Switch from './retro/Switch.js'
  2. Replace the native <select> in the header (lines 202-211) with PermissionSelect. Value is plugin.permission, onChange calls handlePluginPermissionChange which calls setPluginPermission(plugin.name, newPermission).
  3. handlePluginPermissionChange should remain simple — it calls setPluginPermission which sets the plugin-level default. It should NOT iterate tools or batch-write.
  4. Remove handleToggleGroup function (lines 91-104).
  5. Replace the group header (lines 234-244) with a plain div: remove <Switch> and its props, keep just the group name span.

The plugin-level PermissionSelect shows plugin.permission (the default). Tool-level PermissionSelect in ToolRow shows the tool's effective permission. These are independent — the plugin default applies to tools that don't have an explicit override.

userStories.title: Smart tool-level permission: remove override when it matches plugin default
userStories.description: As a developer, I want tool-level permission changes that match the plugin default to remove the per-tool override so that config stays minimal
acceptanceCriteria: When handleToolPermissionChange is called and the new permission matches the plugin-level default, the server removes the per-tool override from pluginPermissions.tools instead of writing it
handleConfigSetToolPermission in extension-handlers.ts: if the new permission equals the plugin-level permission, delete the tool key from the tools map instead of setting it
Config file after setting a tool to the same value as plugin default does NOT contain that tool entry
Example: plugin permission is 'ask', user sets a tool from 'auto' back to 'ask' — the tool entry is removed, not set to 'ask'
Unit test: setToolPermission with permission matching plugin default removes the override
Unit test: setToolPermission with permission differing from plugin default creates the override
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.ts — In handleConfigSetToolPermission (around line 392-397), after determining the new permission, compare it to the plugin-level default: `const pluginDefault = pConfig.permission ?? 'off'`. If `permission === pluginDefault`, delete `tools[tool]` instead of setting it. If the tools map becomes empty, optionally delete it too.
- platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.test.ts — Add tests for the smart cleanup behavior.

This is a server-only change. The side panel UI doesn't need to change — it shows the effective permission (which is the same whether stored as an explicit override or inherited from plugin default).

userStories.title: E2E tests for RetroUI Select permission flow
userStories.description: As a developer, I want E2E tests verifying the new Select-based permission UI works correctly end-to-end
acceptanceCriteria: E2E test: plugin-level Select is visible and shows the current permission value
E2E test: changing plugin-level permission via Select updates the plugin default on the server
E2E test: tool-level Select is visible within expanded plugin accordion
E2E test: changing a tool permission via Select creates a per-tool override on the server
E2E test: setting a tool permission back to the plugin default removes the override (config stays clean)
E2E test: group headers are plain text dividers with no interactive controls
E2E test: skipPermissions mode disables all Select components
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:
- e2e/side-panel-plugin-toggle.e2e.ts — This file tests the permission toggle UI. Update selectors from native <select> to Radix Select triggers. The Radix Select trigger can be located by aria-label. To change the value: click the trigger, then click the desired item in the dropdown.

Playwright interactions for Radix Select:
1. Click the trigger: page.locator('[aria-label="Permission for slack plugin"]').click()
2. Wait for the content to appear: page.locator('[role="option"]')
3. Click the desired option: page.locator('[role="option"]', { hasText: 'Auto' }).click()

Also verify that no Switch components exist in group headers — use page.locator to confirm absence.
